attributes.stem: A 68-year-old man presents for discussion of treatment options following a recent diagnosis of prostate cancer. His prostate specific antigen (PSA) is 12 ng/mL (0-4). Digital rectal examination reveals a 1.5 cm firm nodule in the right posterior lobe. Prostate biopsy shows adenocarcinoma with Gleason score 7 (4+3). Prostate-specific membrane antigen positron emission tomography (PSMA PET) scan shows disease confined to the prostate with no evidence of metastatic disease. His Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status is 0. After discussion of all treatment options, including radical prostatectomy, he expresses a strong preference for a minimally invasive approach.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with intermediate-risk localised prostate cancer based on his PSA level, Gleason score, and clinical stage. While he prefers a minimally invasive approach, oncological outcomes must be prioritised in treatment selection.

External beam radiotherapy (EBRT) represents the best balance between minimally invasive treatment and proven oncological outcomes for intermediate-risk disease. Modern techniques like intensity-modulated radiotherapy (IMRT) allow precise targeting while minimising side effects. The typical course involves 74-78 Gy delivered over 7-8 weeks.

Low dose seed brachytherapy alone is most appropriate for low-risk disease (PSA <10, Gleason score ≤6, and clinical stage T1c-T2a). This patient's intermediate-risk features make him less suitable for brachytherapy monotherapy.

Androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) alone would be inadequate treatment for localised disease where curative intent is appropriate. It is primarily used in combination with radiotherapy for high-risk disease or as palliative therapy for metastatic disease.

High intensity focussed ultrasound (HIFU) is an emerging focal therapy that uses ultrasound energy to ablate prostate tissue. While minimally invasive, it lacks long-term oncological outcome data for intermediate-risk disease and is not yet considered standard of care.

Nano knife irreversible electroporation (IRE) is an experimental focal therapy that uses electrical pulses to destroy cancer cells. Like HIFU, it lacks long-term outcome data and is not yet established as standard treatment for intermediate-risk prostate cancer.

While the patient's preference for minimally invasive treatment is important, the primary consideration must be selecting a treatment with proven oncological efficacy. Novel focal therapies may be promising but currently lack the long-term evidence base of established treatments like external beam radiotherapy.
learningPoints: External beam radiotherapy is appropriate for intermediate-risk localised prostate cancer with a good performance status.
Patient preference for minimally invasive approaches should be balanced against oncological outcomes in cancer patients.
Novel focal therapies like HIFU and IRE are not yet standard of care for intermediate-risk prostate cancer.
references: Prostate adenocarcinoma definitive EBRT conventional intermediate-risk. In: eviQ [online]. 2020. Available at: https://www.eviq.org.au/radiation-oncology/urogenital/prostate/234-prostate-adenocarcinoma-definitive-ebrt-conven#references

Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25072507/
